Strategy Description: A market timing strategy that uses moving averages to gain exposure to asset classes only when they are in an uptrend, aiming to reduce volatility and drawdowns compared to passive buy-and-hold.
Core Logic: Uses a 10-month Simple Moving Average (SMA) as a trend filter to switch between risky assets and risk-free investments (cash).
Entry Conditions: Invest in an asset class ETF when its current price is above its 10-month Simple Moving Average.
Exit Conditions: Exit the asset class and move to cash when its price falls below its 10-month Simple Moving Average.
Risk Management Rules: Binary investment decision (in or out); diversification across 5 major asset classes; exit to cash during downtrends.
Market Regime Suitability: Performs well in trending markets; designed to avoid major bear markets and reduce tail risk.
Volatility Considerations: Significantly reduces volatility and drawdowns compared to equities; achieves equity-like returns with bond-like volatility.
Full Notes: Popularized by Mebane Faber in 'A Quantitative Approach to Tactical Asset Allocation'. The strategy is robust across various markets and can be modified with risk parity weighting.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Simple Moving Average, Trend Following, Tactical Asset Allocation.

Strategy Description: A rotational momentum system that compares the performance of various asset classes and holds only the top-performing ones, rotating holdings monthly.
Core Logic: Exploits relative momentum (relative strength) by picking assets with the highest past 12-month returns.
Entry Conditions: Pick the top 3 ETFs from a universe of 5 (SPY, EFA, BND, VNQ, GSG) based on the strongest 12-month momentum.
Exit Conditions: Rebalance monthly; exit assets that fall out of the top 3 ranking.
Risk Management Rules: Equal weighting of the top 3 assets; monthly rebalancing to ensure only winners are held.
Market Regime Suitability: Exploits different sensitivities of asset classes to business cycles; suitable for multi-asset diversification.
Volatility Considerations: Estimated volatility of 11%; lower drawdown than individual equity indices due to diversification.
Full Notes: Based on Mebane Faber's rotational system. Strong evidence for momentum across asset classes is supported by Kessler and Scherer (2009).
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Relative Momentum, Ranking, Cross-sectional Momentum.

Strategy Description: A strategy that rotates between different industry sectors based on their past performance, aiming to outperform a broad equity index.
Core Logic: Industry components of stock returns exhibit strong momentum; rotating into winning sectors captures this effect.
Entry Conditions: Pick the top 3 sector ETFs (out of 10) with the strongest 12-month momentum.
Exit Conditions: Rebalance monthly; exit sectors that are no longer in the top 3.
Risk Management Rules: Equal weighting of the top 3 sectors; monthly rebalancing.
Long/Short Logic: Long-only (can be long-short if shorting the market index).
Market Regime Suitability: Utilizes different sector sensitivities to business cycles; not a hedge for bear markets as it is 100% equity.
Volatility Considerations: Volatility is comparable to the US equity index (approx. 18.38%).
Full Notes: Based on Mebane Faber's 'Relative Strength Strategies for Investing'. Industry momentum often explains a large part of individual stock momentum.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Momentum, Sector Rotation, Relative Strength.

Strategy Description: A classic currency strategy that sells low-interest-rate currencies and buys high-interest-rate currencies to capture the interest rate differential.
Core Logic: Exploits the 'forward-rate bias' or 'forward premium puzzle', where high-yield currencies do not depreciate enough to offset their interest advantage.
Entry Conditions: Go long the 3 currencies with the highest central bank prime rates and short the 3 with the lowest.
Exit Conditions: Rebalance monthly based on updated interest rates.
Indicators Used: Central Bank Prime Rates / 3-Month Interbank Rates.
Risk Management Rules: Diversification across multiple currency pairs; cash not used as margin is invested in overnight rates.
Market Regime Suitability: Performs well in stable global environments; susceptible to 'carry crashes' during liquidity crises.
Volatility Considerations: Estimated volatility of 9.6%; strongly correlated to the business cycle.
Full Notes: One of the most well-known and profitable FX strategies. Returns are often linked to global risk appetite.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Uncovered Interest Rate Parity (UIP), Forward Premium Puzzle.

Strategy Name: Low Volatility Factor Effect in Stocks
Strategy Description: A strategy that invests in stocks with the lowest historical volatility, challenging the CAPM assumption that higher risk leads to higher returns.
Core Logic: Low-risk stocks exhibit significantly higher risk-adjusted returns than the market portfolio, while high-risk stocks underperform.
Entry Conditions: Rank stocks by their past 3-year volatility of weekly returns and go long the top decile (lowest volatility).
Exit Conditions: Rebalance monthly.
Risk Management Rules: Focus on low-beta/low-volatility stocks; can be implemented as long-short to hedge equity risk.
Market Regime Suitability: Defensive strategy; performs well during market turmoil and bear markets.
Volatility Considerations: Achieves approx. 25% volatility reduction compared to the market index.
Full Notes: Based on Blitz and van Vliet (2007). The effect is attributed to leverage restrictions and behavioral biases (lottery-seeking).
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Standard Deviation, Volatility, Risk-Adjusted Returns, CAPM.

Strategy Description: A trend-following strategy in the FX market that buys currencies that have appreciated in the past and sells those that have depreciated.
Core Logic: Exchange rates tend to trend on a multi-year basis due to investor underreaction to news and market segmentation.
Entry Conditions: Go long the 3 currencies with the highest 12-month momentum against USD and short the 3 with the lowest.
Exit Conditions: Rebalance monthly.
Risk Management Rules: Monthly rebalancing; diversification across 10-20 currencies.
Market Regime Suitability: Hedge for global economic risk; spread is larger in high return dispersion (crisis) states.
Volatility Considerations: Estimated volatility of 10.22%; high drawdown potential (-45.87%).
Full Notes: Documented by Menkhoff et al. (2012). Distinct from carry trade as it is driven by spot rate continuation.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Momentum, Cross-sectional Spread, Return Dispersion.

Strategy Description: A value-based FX strategy that buys 'undervalued' currencies and sells 'overvalued' ones based on Purchasing Power Parity (PPP).
Core Logic: Currencies tend to move towards their fair value in the long run; price differences narrow over time through exchange rate movements.
Entry Conditions: Go long the 3 most undervalued currencies (lowest PPP fair value figure) and short the 3 most overvalued.
Exit Conditions: Rebalance quarterly or monthly.
Indicators Used: OECD Purchasing Power Parity (PPP), CPI changes.
Risk Management Rules: Quarterly rebalancing; diversification across 10-20 currencies.
Market Regime Suitability: Valuable hedge during stress; low correlation to traditional equity factors.
Full Notes: Based on the Law of One Price. Refined valuation measures relate closely to macroeconomic fundamentals.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Purchasing Power Parity, Real Exchange Rates, Fair Value.

Strategy Description: A statistical arbitrage strategy that identifies pairs of stocks that move together and trades the spread when it deviates from its historical mean.
Core Logic: Assumes that cointegrated stock prices will converge after temporary shocks move them out of their common price band.
Entry Conditions: Open a long-short position when the normalized price series of a pair diverges by more than 2 standard deviations.
Exit Conditions: Close the position when the prices revert to the mean.
Indicators Used: Sum of squared deviations (Distance), Standard Deviation of the spread.
Risk Management Rules: Pairs formed over 12 months; top 20 pairs with smallest historical distance are traded; illiquid stocks removed.
Market Regime Suitability: Performs well during market crises and high volatility; 'liquidity providing' strategy.
Full Notes: Pioneered by Nunzio Tartaglia. Profits are psychological (investor over-reaction) and fundamental (cointegration).
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Cointegration, Distance Measure, Mean Reversion, Standard Deviation.

Strategy Description: A strategy that exploits the tendency of stocks with low returns over the past week/month to earn positive abnormal returns in the following period.
Core Logic: Investor overreaction to past information leads to a short-term correction.
Entry Conditions: Go long the 10 stocks with the lowest performance in the previous week and short the 10 with the greatest performance in the prior month.
Exit Conditions: Rebalance weekly.
Risk Management Rules: Focus on large-cap stocks to reduce transaction costs; strict risk management during high volatility.
Market Regime Suitability: Liquidity providing; performs well during market crises and weeks following large declines.
Volatility Considerations: Estimated volatility of 14.94%; high drawdown potential (-52.94%).
Full Notes: Documented by DeBondt and Thaler. Net profits are significant among large-cap stocks where transaction costs are lower.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Overreaction, Liquidity Provision, Mean Reversion.

Strategy Description: A strategy that buys stocks that have performed well in the past and sells those that have performed poorly, expecting the trend to continue.
Core Logic: Stocks that outperformed peers over a 3-12 month period tend to continue to perform well in the upcoming period.
Entry Conditions: Go long stocks with high relative past 12-month returns (skipping the most recent month) and short those with low returns.
Exit Conditions: Rebalance monthly.
Risk Management Rules: Managing time-varying specific risk can eliminate crashes and double the Sharpe ratio.
Market Regime Suitability: Prone to 'momentum crashes' during market rebounds following large declines.
Volatility Considerations: Estimated volatility of 16.6%; very high drawdown potential (-87.41%).
Full Notes: Based on Jegadeesh and Titman (1993). Behavioral explanations include herding and over/underreaction.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Momentum, Relative Strength, Decile Ranking.

Strategy Name: Momentum Factor Effect in Country Equity Indexes
Strategy Description: A trend-following strategy that buys country equity indexes (via ETFs) that have shown the best past performance.
Core Logic: Country indices show more persistent momentum than single shares because they are less likely to be 'traded-out'.
Entry Conditions: Pick the top 5 countries with the best 10-12 month momentum.
Exit Conditions: Rebalance monthly.
Risk Management Rules: Monthly rebalancing; geographically diversified assets.
Market Regime Suitability: Higher equity beta during late business cycles; falls faster than benchmark during rebounds.
Full Notes: Based on Muller and Ward (2010). Extra returns are larger for emerging markets than developed markets.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Momentum, Relative Strength, Country Selection.

Strategy Name: Mean Reversion Effect in Country Equity Indexes
Strategy Description: A long-term contrarian strategy that buys country equity indexes that have been 'losers' over a 3-year horizon.
Core Logic: Relative stock index prices exhibit mean reversion with a half-life of approximately 3 to 3.5 years.
Entry Conditions: Go long the bottom 4 countries with the worst 36-month return and short the top 4 with the best.
Exit Conditions: Rebalance every 3 years.
Risk Management Rules: 3-year rebalancing; diversification across 16+ countries.
Market Regime Suitability: Works best after crises; not a good hedge during crises due to high equity exposure.
Full Notes: Documented by Richards (1995). Reversals are stronger among smaller markets.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Mean Reversion, Half-life, Contrarian Investing.

Strategy Description: A strategy that earns a systematic risk premium by selling at-the-money options, exploiting the fact that implied volatility is usually higher than realized volatility.
Core Logic: Investors pay a premium for portfolio insurance (puts), leading to overpriced options.
Entry Conditions: Sell at-the-money straddle (1 month maturity) and buy 15% out-of-the-money puts as insurance.
Exit Conditions: Rebalance monthly.
Risk Management Rules: Buy OTM puts for crash protection; substantial margin reserves required.
Market Regime Suitability: Fat-tail strategy; loses heavily during crisis periods; not a hedge.
Volatility Considerations: Estimated volatility of 19%; return distribution is very abnormal.
Full Notes: Selling puts is generally more profitable than selling calls. Returns are compensation for bearing tail risk.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Implied Volatility, Variance Risk Premium, Peso Problem.

Strategy Description: A strategy that buys commodity futures that have outperformed in the recent past and sells those that have underperformed.
Core Logic: Short-term price continuation exists in commodity markets, often linked to backwardation and contango states.
Entry Conditions: Rank commodity futures by 12-month performance and go long the top quintile and short the bottom quintile.
Exit Conditions: Rebalance monthly.
Risk Management Rules: Monthly rebalancing; trades liquid contracts with nearby maturities.
Market Regime Suitability: Excellent candidate for an uncorrelated strategy; often acts as a hedge to equity market factor.
Volatility Considerations: Estimated volatility of 25.57%; high drawdown potential (-79.75%).
Full Notes: Based on Miffre and Rallis (2007). Momentum returns have low correlations with traditional asset classes.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Momentum, Quintile Ranking, Normal Backwardation.

Strategy Description: A strategy that exploits the roll yield in commodity futures by buying backwardated contracts and shorting contangoed contracts.
Core Logic: Commodity futures prices depend on the net positions of hedgers; speculators earn a return for taking on price fluctuation risk.
Entry Conditions: Buy the 20% of commodities with the highest roll-returns and short the 20% with the lowest.
Exit Conditions: Rebalance monthly.
Risk Management Rules: Equal weighting in each quintile; monthly rebalancing.
Market Regime Suitability: Unrelated to S&P500; offers diversification for equity portfolios.
Volatility Considerations: Estimated volatility of 23.84%; high drawdown potential (-78.06%).
Full Notes: Based on Keynes (1930) and Cootner (1960). Term-structure strategies often have higher reward-to-risk than passive benchmarks.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Backwardation, Contango, Roll Yield, Principal Component Analysis.

Strategy Name: Size Factor - Small Capitalization Stocks Premium
Strategy Description: A strategy that exploits the 'size effect' by investing in stocks with low market capitalization, which historically earn premiums over large-cap stocks.
Core Logic: Small companies have more space to grow and higher flexibility, but also higher illiquidity and risk.
Entry Conditions: Form decile portfolios based on market cap; go long the lowest decile (small) and short the highest decile (big).
Exit Conditions: Rebalance yearly.
Risk Management Rules: Yearly rebalancing; can be implemented as long-only to avoid disastrous drawdowns.
Market Regime Suitability: Performs poorly leading up to crises; performs well after economic crises.
Volatility Considerations: Estimated volatility of 25.6%; high drawdown potential (-39.68%).
Full Notes: Documented by Fama and French (1993). The effect is dismantled when controlling for 'junk' (quality).
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Size Effect, SMB (Small Minus Big), Decile Ranking.

Strategy Description: A strategy that buys stocks with high book-to-market ratios (fundamentally cheap) and sells those with low ratios (growth/expensive).
Core Logic: Investors overreact to growth aspects, leading to undervaluation of value stocks.
Entry Conditions: Go long high book-to-price stocks and short low book-to-price stocks (HML portfolio).
Exit Conditions: Rebalance monthly.
Risk Management Rules: Monthly rebalancing; average of small and large cap HML portfolios.
Market Regime Suitability: Value stocks are generally less sensitive to major stock market declines than growth stocks.
Volatility Considerations: Estimated volatility of 12.02%; high drawdown potential (-55.99%).
Full Notes: Based on Fama and French (1993). Value premium is linked to liquidity and growth risk.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Value Premium, HML (High Minus Low), Book-to-Market Ratio.

Strategy Name: Value and Momentum Factors across Asset Classes
Strategy Description: A global tactical asset allocation strategy that combines value and momentum signals across multiple asset classes to enhance profitability.
Core Logic: Value and momentum are negatively correlated within and across asset classes; combining them increases robustness.
Entry Conditions: Rank asset classes by 12m momentum (25%), 1m momentum (25%), and valuation (50%). Go long top quartile, short bottom.
Exit Conditions: Rebalance monthly.
Indicators Used: Price Momentum, Adjusted Yield (E/P for stocks, YTM for bonds).
Risk Management Rules: Signal weighting (25/25/50); market-neutral exposure.
Market Regime Suitability: Good hedging/diversification abilities in times of market stress (high VIX).
Full Notes: Based on Blitz and van Vliet (2008). Returns cannot be explained by implicit beta exposures.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Multi-factor Ranking, Z-score, Tactical Asset Allocation.

Strategy Name: Market Seasonality Effect in World Equity Indexes
Strategy Description: A market timing strategy based on the 'Sell in May and go away' maxim, holding equities only during the winter months.
Core Logic: Stocks deliver a risk premium from November through April and close to zero returns from May through October.
Entry Conditions: Be invested in global equity markets during the November - April period.
Exit Conditions: Stay in cash during the May - October period.
Risk Management Rules: Exit to cash for 6 months of the year; reduces overall equity risk.
Market Regime Suitability: Market timing overlay to manage equity representation; decreases overall risk.
Volatility Considerations: Reduces volatility and drawdowns compared to buy-and-hold.
Full Notes: Attributed to the SAD (Seasonal Affective Disorder) effect or an optimism cycle at the start of the year.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Seasonality, Calendar Effects, Market Timing.

Strategy Description: A strategy that exploits the tendency for a stock's cumulative abnormal returns to drift for several weeks following a positive earnings announcement.
Core Logic: Investors under-react to earnings news, leading to a gradual incorporation of information into prices.
Entry Conditions: Go long stocks in the intersection of top SUE (Standardized Unexpected Earnings) and EAR (Earnings Announcement Return) quintiles.
Exit Conditions: Hold for one quarter (60 working days).
Indicators Used: SUE (Standardized Unexpected Earnings), EAR (Earnings Announcement Return).
Risk Management Rules: Quarterly rebalancing; avoid financial/utility firms and low-priced stocks.
Market Regime Suitability: Short leg can potentially act as a hedge during bad times; long leg is strongly correlated to the market.
Volatility Considerations: Strongest in small-cap stocks; caution recommended due to liquidity risk.
Full Notes: First discovered by Ball and Brown (1968). EAR captures market reaction to unexpected information beyond just earnings.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: PEAD (Post-Earnings Announcement Drift), SUE, Abnormal Returns.

Category: Value, Fundamental Analysis, Factor Investing, Smart Beta
Strategy Description: A strategy that invests in stocks with high net payout yields (dividends plus repurchases minus issuances) and shorts those with low yields, based on the premise that total shareholder yield is a better predictor of returns than dividend yield alone.
Core Logic: Dividend yield has lost its predictive power as companies increasingly use stock repurchases to distribute cash. Net payout yield captures the total cash returned to shareholders, which remains a strong predictor of future performance.
Entry Conditions: At the end of June each year, rank stocks by net payout yield. Form ten portfolios (deciles). Buy the portfolio with the highest net payout yield.
Exit Conditions: Hold the portfolio for one year and rebalance annually.
Indicators Used: Net Payout Yield = (Dividends + Repurchases - Common Share Issuances) / Market Capitalization.
Risk Management Rules: Annual rebalancing; diversification across approximately 1000 instruments.
Long/Short Logic: Can be implemented as long-only (highest yield decile) or long-short (long high yield, short low yield).
Market Regime Suitability: Not suitable as a hedge for bear markets; high correlation to equity market premium.
Performance Metrics (CAGR, Sharpe Ratio,: CAGR: 22.13%, Sharpe Ratio: Not stated, Max Drawdown: -52.5%
Full Notes: Investment universe includes NYSE, AMEX, and NASDAQ stocks. The strategy shows negative market betas and negative loading on the size factor.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Net Payout Yield calculation, Fama-MacBeth regressions, Cross-sectional time-series analysis.

Category: Value, Fundamental Analysis, Factor Investing, Smart Beta
Strategy Description: A deep value strategy popularized by Benjamin Graham that buys stocks trading at a significant discount to their net current asset value (liquidation value).
Core Logic: Stocks with market capitalization lower than their net current assets (cash + inventories - all liabilities) are often in distress but statistically provide high positive returns as a portfolio.
Entry Conditions: Select stocks with a Net Current Asset Value to Market Value (NCAV/MV) ratio higher than 1.5.
Exit Conditions: Hold the buy-and-hold portfolio for one year.
Indicators Used: NCAV/MV = (Current Assets - Total Liabilities) / Market Value.
Risk Management Rules: Equal weighting; annual rebalancing; exclusion of financial sector and foreign companies.
Market Regime Suitability: Not a hedge for bear markets; high correlation to equity market premium.
Performance Metrics (CAGR, Sharpe Ratio,: CAGR: 31.19%, Sharpe Ratio: Not stated, Max Drawdown: -76.86%
Full Notes: Universe: London Exchange. Excludes companies with multiple share classes and those on lightly regulated markets.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: NCAV calculation, CAPM, Fama-French three-factor model analysis.

Category: Equity Long Short, Fundamental Analysis, Accruals Effect, Factor Investing, Smart Beta
Strategy Description: A strategy that exploits the negative association between accounting accruals and subsequent stock returns by going long on low-accrual stocks and short on high-accrual stocks.
Core Logic: Investors often fixate on earnings and fail to distinguish between the cash-flow and accrual components. High accruals often indicate lower quality earnings or accounting manipulation, leading to subsequent underperformance.
Entry Conditions: Sort stocks into deciles based on balance sheet accruals. Go long the lowest decile and short the highest decile.
Exit Conditions: Rebalance the portfolio yearly in May after earnings publications.
Indicators Used: BS_ACC = (∆Current Assets - ∆Cash) - (∆Current Liabilities - ∆Short-term Debt - ∆Income Taxes Payable) - Depreciation.
Risk Management Rules: Yearly rebalancing; diversification across approximately 1000 instruments.
Market Regime Suitability: Unknown; long leg is correlated to equity market, short leg may provide some diversification.
Full Notes: Universe: NYSE, AMEX, NASDAQ. Recent research suggests the anomaly may be split into risk-based investment accruals and mispriced non-investment accruals.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Accruals calculation, Earnings fixation hypothesis, Intertemporal CAPM.

Strategy Name: Turn of the Month in Equity Indexes
Strategy Description: A seasonal strategy that buys equity indexes at the end of the month and sells them after the first few trading days of the next month, capturing the period where most market returns occur.
Core Logic: Historically, almost all excess market returns are accrued during a four-day turn-of-the-month period, likely due to month-end cash flows, pension fund reinvestments, and portfolio rebalancing.
Entry Conditions: Buy the equity index (e.g., SPY ETF) 1 trading day before the end of the month.
Exit Conditions: Sell on the 3rd trading day of the new month at the close.
Risk Management Rules: Market timing; remains in cash for the majority of the month (approx. 16 days).
Market Regime Suitability: Not a hedge for bear markets; timing equity market factor.
Full Notes: The effect is persistent across 30 different markets and is not confined to small-cap stocks or specific quarter-ends.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Calendar seasonality, ARMA (1,1), EGARCH (1,1), Percentile-t-bootstrap.

Strategy Description: A strategy that mimics the 'best ideas' or high-conviction stock picks of top-performing mutual and hedge fund managers as disclosed in their SEC 13F filings.
Core Logic: Active managers often have skill in picking individual stocks but are forced to diversify. Their top holdings represent their highest conviction and tend to outperform the market.
Entry Conditions: Identify 'best idea' stocks (top holdings) from 13F filings of a selected universe of active managers. Invest in stocks that appear as 'best ideas' for multiple managers.
Exit Conditions: Rebalance quarterly following new 13F disclosures.
Risk Management Rules: Diversification across 10-100 instruments.
Market Regime Suitability: Not a hedge for bear markets; high correlation to broad equity market.
Performance Metrics (CAGR, Sharpe Ratio,: CAGR: 20.21%, Sharpe Ratio: 1.8, Max Drawdown: Not stated
Full Notes: Copycat funds can often outperform the original funds after accounting for expenses. Performance may have declined slightly post-2008.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Feature extraction, Deep Reinforcement Learning, Alpha generation.

Category: Momentum, Asset Class Picking, Rotational System, Market Timing, Fund Picking
Strategy Description: A rotational strategy that switches between two negatively correlated assets (e.g., stocks and bonds) based on their relative performance over a recent period.
Core Logic: Exploits the negative correlation between asset classes by holding the one with the strongest recent momentum, aiming to improve risk-adjusted returns over a static mix.
Entry Conditions: Compare the performance of two assets (e.g., VFINX equity fund and VUSTX bond fund) over the prior quarter. Buy the asset with the higher return.
Exit Conditions: Hold for one quarter and repeat the comparison.
Risk Management Rules: Quarterly rebalancing; rotation into defensive assets during equity stress.
Market Regime Suitability: Partially suitable as a hedge; tactical allocation rotates out of equities during stress.
Full Notes: Simple criteria for switching can lead to lower volatility without significant return penalties. Works well with passive ETFs.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Negative correlation, Relative pricing, Relative volatility, Trend following.

Category: Stock Picking, Equity Long Short, Factor Investing, Smart Beta
Strategy Description: A strategy that goes long on stocks with low short interest and short on stocks with high short interest, based on the predictive power of short sellers' information.
Core Logic: Short sellers are often informed traders. High short interest indicates overvaluation or hidden bad news, while low short interest suggests market agreement that a stock is not overvalued.
Entry Conditions: Each month, sort stocks into deciles based on the ratio of short interest to shares outstanding. Long the lowest decile, short the highest decile.
Exit Conditions: Monthly rebalancing.
Indicators Used: Short Interest Ratio = Short Interest / Shares Outstanding.
Risk Management Rules: Equal weighting; monthly rebalancing.
Market Regime Suitability: Unknown; low correlation to overall market suggests potential as a diversifier.
Full Notes: Short sellers contribute to price discovery about firm fundamentals. The strategy has a low correlation to the overall market.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Overvaluation hypothesis, Information hypothesis, Four-factor model alpha.

Strategy Name: Short Interest Effect - Long Only Version
Category: Stock Picking, Factor Investing, Smart Beta
Strategy Description: A strategy that holds a portfolio of stocks with the lowest levels of short interest, benefiting from the 'good news' implied by the absence of short sellers.
Core Logic: Stocks avoided by short sellers are likely not overvalued. Positive information associated with low short interest is incorporated into prices slowly, creating an exploitable anomaly.
Entry Conditions: Sort stocks by short interest ratio and hold the first percentile (lowest short interest).
Exit Conditions: Monthly rebalancing.
Risk Management Rules: Equal weighting; monthly rebalancing.
Market Regime Suitability: Not a hedge for bear markets; high correlation to equity market premium.
Performance Metrics (CAGR, Sharpe Ratio,: CAGR: 26.8%, Sharpe Ratio: Not stated, Max Drawdown: -33.29%
Full Notes: The strategy can be easily implemented and maintains attractive parameters like a small market beta.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Information hypothesis, Market efficiency, Short-sale constraints.

Strategy Description: A market timing strategy that allocates between stocks and cash based on the yield gap between the equity market's earnings yield and long-term bond yields.
Core Logic: Stocks and bonds are competing assets. When the earnings yield of stocks is significantly higher than bond yields, stocks are considered undervalued and expected to outperform.
Entry Conditions: Conduct a monthly predictive regression of excess stock returns on the yield gap. If forecasted excess returns are positive, allocate 100% to stocks.
Exit Conditions: If forecasted excess returns are negative, allocate 100% to the risk-free rate (cash).
Indicators Used: Yield Gap = Earnings Yield (ln(1 + E/P)) - Log 10-year Treasury Bond Yield.
Risk Management Rules: Binary allocation (100% stocks or 100% cash); monthly rebalancing.
Market Regime Suitability: Partially suitable; rotates out of equities during stress based on relative valuation.
Full Notes: The model is controversial; critics argue it compares real yields (stocks) to nominal yields (bonds). It works best at short-term horizons.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Predictive regression, Cointegration, ICAPM, Dynamic accounting identity.

Category: Stock Picking, Equity Long Short, Fundamental Analysis, Factor Investing, Smart Beta
Strategy Description: A strategy that goes long on stocks with low asset growth and short on stocks with high asset growth, exploiting the tendency of high-growth firms to underperform.
Core Logic: Low asset growth stocks significantly outperform high asset growth stocks. This may be due to risk compensation (growth options vs. assets in place) or market mispricing (extrapolation of past gains).
Entry Conditions: At the end of June, sort stocks into deciles based on the percentage change in total assets from the previous year. Long the lowest decile, short the highest decile.
Exit Conditions: Yearly rebalancing.
Risk Management Rules: Equal weighting; yearly rebalancing; exclusion of financial stocks.
Market Regime Suitability: Unknown; low correlation to equity market factor.
Full Notes: Asset growth is as powerful as size or momentum in explaining returns. The effect is stronger in developed markets.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: q-theory, Extrapolation, Representativeness heuristic, Fama-MacBeth regressions.

Strategy Description: Pairs trading on country ETFs where the trader buys and simultaneously sells two ETFs that follow each other when they diverge from their normal synchronized moves.
Core Logic: Country ETFs are often cointegrated due to shared fundamental return correlations. Temporary shocks create statistical arbitrage opportunities when pairs diverge from their historical price band.
Entry Conditions: Divergence between the normalized price series of a pair exceeds 0.5x the historical standard deviation.
Exit Conditions: The pair converges or a 20-day holding period is reached.
Indicators Used: Normalized cumulative total return index, Sum of squared deviations (Distance), Standard Deviation.
Risk Management Rules: Top 5 pairs with the smallest distance are selected; pairs are weighted equally; daily monitoring and rebalancing.
Long/Short Logic: Long the undervalued ETF and short the overvalued ETF.
Market Regime Suitability: Market neutral strategy, though it may show small positive correlation to equity markets.
Volatility Considerations: Estimated annualized daily volatility of approximately 10%.
Full Notes: The strategy uses a 120-day formation period to identify the top 5 pairs with the smallest distance. It is more resilient than stock pairs trading due to the diversified nature of ETFs.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Cointegration, Statistical Arbitrage, Price Normalization, Sum of Squared Deviations.

Strategy Description: A strategy that identifies and rides industry bubbles, exiting when the bubble starts to pop.
Core Logic: Bubbles are defined as structural breaks in an industry's alpha return compared to the market. Excess returns during the bubble phase often offset the risk of the subsequent crash.
Entry Conditions: Industry alpha is statistically significant at a 97.5% threshold based on the CAPM model (or Fama/French 3-factor model) using 10 years of past data.
Exit Conditions: Alpha is no longer statistically significant or monthly rebalancing occurs.
Risk Management Rules: 1/N rule (equal weighting) among all industries experiencing a bubble; no investment if no bubble is detected.
Market Regime Suitability: Bull markets and bubble regimes; not suitable as a hedge for bear markets.
Volatility Considerations: Not explicitly stated, but involves high risk due to bubble dynamics.
Full Notes: Distinct from standard industry momentum; bubbles are identified by abnormal returns that cannot be explained by standard risk factors.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: CAPM, Regression Analysis, Statistical Significance, Structural Breaks.

Strategy Description: A contrarian strategy in the futures market that buys past losers and sells past winners, filtered by trading volume and open interest.
Core Logic: Market overreaction leads to price reversals. High trading volume and low open interest are indicators of irrationality-induced inefficiencies that precede reversals.
Entry Conditions: Contracts in the high-volume, low-open interest group with the lowest (long) or greatest (short) returns in the previous week.
Exit Conditions: Weekly rebalancing.
Indicators Used: Weekly Returns, Detrended Trading Volume, Changes in Open Interest.
Risk Management Rules: Contract weights are proportional to the difference between the contract's return and the equal-weighted average return of the group.
Long/Short Logic: Long past losers, short past winners (Contrarian).
Market Regime Suitability: Liquidity providing; often performs well during market crises but carries short-volatility risks.
Full Notes: Uses 24 types of US futures contracts. Volume is detrended by dividing by its sample mean to ensure comparability across markets.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Overreaction Hypothesis, Detrending, Median Filtering, Proportional Weighting.

Strategy Name: Federal Open Market Committee Meeting Effect in Stocks
Strategy Description: A market timing strategy that goes long on stocks only during Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting days.
Core Logic: FOMC meetings are generally positive for the market as the Fed aims to maintain stability and growth, leading to a positive drift in stock prices.
Entry Conditions: Go long (e.g., S&P 500 ETF) at the close one day before the FOMC meeting.
Exit Conditions: Close the position at the end of the meeting day.
Risk Management Rules: Invested in cash during all non-meeting days; low market exposure (approx. 8 days per year).
Market Regime Suitability: Not a hedge for bear markets; exploits specific event-driven optimism.
Volatility Considerations: Low overall portfolio volatility due to high cash allocation.
Full Notes: More than 16% of average yearly returns for equity indices are realized during these few days. The strategy can be easily leveraged.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Calendar Effect, Drift Analysis, Event Study.

Strategy Name: Betting Against Beta Factor in Stocks
Strategy Description: A strategy that goes long on low-beta stocks and short on high-beta stocks, using leverage to neutralize market exposure.
Core Logic: Leverage-constrained investors over-weigh high-beta stocks to achieve higher returns, making them overvalued. Low-beta stocks deliver higher risk-adjusted returns.
Entry Conditions: Rank stocks by beta; long the low-beta decile and short the high-beta decile.
Exit Conditions: Monthly rebalancing.
Indicators Used: Beta (calculated using a 1-year rolling window against the market index).
Risk Management Rules: Both portfolios are rescaled to have a beta of one at formation to create a zero-beta portfolio.
Market Regime Suitability: Partial hedge during market turmoil; low-beta stocks are generally safer.
Full Notes: The effect is strongest in small-cap stocks. Caution is needed regarding transaction costs and slippage.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Beta, Leverage Constraints, Risk-Adjusted Returns, Zero-Beta Portfolio Construction.

Strategy Name: Betting Against Beta Factor in International Equities
Category: Equities, Long/Short, Country Picking, Beta Factor
Strategy Description: Applying the 'Betting Against Beta' principle to country equity indexes using ETFs or futures.
Core Logic: Similar to the stock-level BAB, investors' leverage constraints lead to the overvaluation of high-beta countries.
Entry Conditions: Rank country ETFs by beta relative to the MSCI US Equity Index; assign to low-beta and high-beta portfolios.
Exit Conditions: Monthly rebalancing.
Risk Management Rules: Portfolios are rescaled to a beta of one; zero-cost, zero-beta construction.
Long/Short Logic: Long low-beta countries, short high-beta countries.
Market Regime Suitability: Partial hedge against equity risk; low-beta countries are safer during turmoil.
Full Notes: The investment universe typically consists of around 13-23 country ETFs. Rebalancing is done every calendar month.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Beta, Leverage Constraints, Portfolio Rescaling, Zero-Beta.

Strategy Description: Exploiting the rise in stock prices around earnings announcement dates, particularly for stocks with high past announcement volume.
Core Logic: Buying pressure from individual investors increases around announcement dates when the news catches their attention, driving prices higher.
Entry Conditions: Rank stocks by volume concentration ratio; long high-volume expected announcers and short high-volume expected non-announcers.
Exit Conditions: Monthly rebalancing.
Indicators Used: Volume Concentration Ratio (past 16 announcement months volume / total 48 months volume).
Risk Management Rules: Stocks are value-weighted within portfolios; rebalanced monthly.
Long/Short Logic: Long high-volume announcers, short high-volume non-announcers.
Market Regime Suitability: Unknown; long leg is correlated to the market, short leg may act as a partial hedge.
Full Notes: The premium is strong in large-cap stocks and has been consistent since 1927. It is unrelated to standard market risk factors.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Volume Concentration, Attention Bias, Value-Weighting.

Strategy Description: A calendar anomaly strategy that goes long on the stock market on the final trading day before a holiday.
Core Logic: Behavioral factors such as short-sellers closing positions and general investor optimism around holidays drive positive market moves.
Entry Conditions: Go long on the day preceding a major public holiday (e.g., New Year's, Christmas, Thanksgiving).
Exit Conditions: Close the position at the end of the pre-holiday trading day.
Risk Management Rules: Stay in cash during all other trading days.
Market Regime Suitability: Not suitable as a hedge; timing-based long exposure.
Volatility Considerations: Low overall portfolio volatility due to limited market exposure.
Full Notes: The effect is documented globally. Pre-holiday returns are often ten times larger than average daily returns.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Calendar Anomaly, Behavioral Finance, Short Covering.

Strategy Description: A strategy that selects mutual funds based on their past performance, exploiting the momentum effect in fund returns.
Core Logic: Investors tend to chase performance, and there is a strong momentum effect between equity styles and management strategies.
Entry Conditions: Sort no-load equity funds by past 6-month return; select the top decile.
Exit Conditions: Quarterly rebalancing.
Risk Management Rules: Equal weighting of selected funds; universe limited to no-load funds to avoid fees.
Market Regime Suitability: Not suitable as a hedge; long-only momentum exposure.
Full Notes: Other measures like closeness to 1-year high in NAV and momentum factor loading also provide independent predictive power.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Performance Chasing, Style Momentum, Decile Sorting.

Strategy Description: A strategy that buys stocks close to their 52-week highs and shorts those far from them, focusing on industry-level information.
Core Logic: Investors use the 52-week high as an anchor and under-react to positive news as prices approach this level, leading to subsequent continuation.
Entry Conditions: Calculate the ratio of current price to 52-week high; long stocks in the 6 industries with the highest weighted average ratios and short the 6 lowest.
Exit Conditions: 3-month holding period.
Indicators Used: Price / 52-Week High Ratio, Industry Market Capitalization.
Risk Management Rules: Equal weighting; monthly rebalancing of 1/3 of the portfolio; avoid implementation in January.
Market Regime Suitability: Unknown; independent of standard momentum effect.
Full Notes: The effect is mainly driven by under-reaction to industry information rather than firm-specific news.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Anchoring Bias, Under-reaction, Weighted Averages, Industry Momentum.

Strategy Name: Momentum Factor and Style Rotation Effect
Category: Equities, Momentum, Rotational System, Factor Allocation
Strategy Description: This strategy applies momentum to rotate between equity styles (small-cap value, large-cap growth, etc.). It leverages the fact that momentum strategies generate excess returns across various asset classes and applies this to factor portfolios.
Core Logic: Equity styles perform differently over time. The popularity of style investing influences asset return dynamics, creating non-random behavior and exploitable momentum as prices deviate from fundamental values.
Entry Conditions: Calculate the 12-month momentum for six Russell equity styles (small-cap value, mid-cap value, large-cap value, small-cap growth, mid-cap growth, large-cap growth). Go long on the style with the highest momentum (the winner).
Exit Conditions: Rebalance the portfolio monthly. Go short on the style with the lowest 12-month momentum (the loser).
Risk Management Rules: The long-short version of the strategy has practically zero correlation to the broad equity market, serving as a portfolio diversifier.
Long/Short Logic: Long on the winner style, short on the loser style.
Market Regime Suitability: Unknown correlation to equity market risk; however, the short leg may act as a hedge during market crises.
Full Notes: Indicative performance is for the long-short strategy. Portfolio reconstruction is required on average every six months.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Relative momentum, tactical asset allocation, risk-adjusted returns, style index momentum.

Strategy Description: A market timing system that uses crude oil prices as a predictor for equity returns. Higher oil prices generally predict lower future equity returns and vice versa.
Core Logic: Based on the underreaction hypothesis; investors take time to fully assess and reflect the impact of oil price changes on stock values.
Entry Conditions: Use monthly oil returns in a regression model to predict stock market returns. If the expected return is higher than the risk-free rate, invest fully in the market portfolio.
Exit Conditions: If the expected stock market return is lower than the risk-free rate, exit the market and invest in cash.
Indicators Used: Monthly oil returns (Brent, WTI, etc.), regression model, risk-free rate.
Risk Management Rules: Rotates out of equities into cash during predicted bear markets.
Market Regime Suitability: Partially hedges during bear markets by rotating to cash.
Full Notes: Performance is compared to a 10.7% benchmark return. The model is re-estimated every month with new data.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Regression analysis, underreaction hypothesis, delayed information diffusion, variance risk premium.

Category: Commodities, Pairs Trading, Arbitrage, Spread Trading
Strategy Description: Exploits the price difference between West Texas Intermediate (WTI) and Brent Crude oil. The spread is mean-reverting because price shocks are typically temporal.
Core Logic: WTI and Brent are chemically similar but differ in production and transportation. The spread moves back to long-term economic equilibrium after deviations.
Entry Conditions: Calculate a 20-day SMA of the WTI/Brent spread. If the current spread is above the SMA, enter a short position in the spread (Short WTI, Long Brent). If below the SMA, enter a long position (Long WTI, Short Brent).
Exit Conditions: Close the short position when the spread crosses below the SMA. Close the long position when the spread crosses above the SMA.
Risk Management Rules: Mean reversion strategy; caution is advised as alpha may deteriorate in out-of-sample periods.
Market Regime Suitability: Unknown; commodities often have negative correlation to equities, potentially providing diversification.
Full Notes: Indicative performance is a weighted average of in-sample and out-of-sample periods. Out-of-sample performance has shown signs of deterioration.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Mean reversion, moving averages, cointegration, ARMA models, Hidden Markov Models.

Category: Equities, Market Timing, Seasonality, Stock Picking
Strategy Description: Large-cap stocks with active options tend to have higher average weekly returns during the week before options expiration (the 3rd Friday of the month).
Core Logic: Delta-hedge rebalancing by option market makers. As options approach expiration, a reduction in call-open interest leads market makers to buy back stocks to close their short-stock hedge positions.
Entry Conditions: Go long on S&P 100 stocks (or a representative ETF) at the beginning of the option-expiration week.
Exit Conditions: Exit the position and stay in cash during all other weeks of the month.
Risk Management Rules: Market timing strategy that limits exposure to only one week per month.
Market Regime Suitability: Not suitable as a hedge during market crises as it remains long-only in equities.
Full Notes: Performance includes the expected return on the cash position held during non-expiration weeks.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Delta hedging, open interest, market maker behavior, idiosyncratic volatility puzzle.

Category: Equities, Stock Picking, Reversal, Arbitrage, Short Selling, Alternative Data
Strategy Description: Exploits inefficiencies in publicly listed soccer club stocks where investors systematically overvalue teams before important matches due to sentiment bias.
Core Logic: Investors are overly optimistic about match outcomes. Bookmaker odds are compiled by experts and are more efficient, while equity prices reflect biased investor sentiment.
Entry Conditions: Sell short stocks of soccer clubs playing UEFA Championship or other important matches at the end of the business day before the match.
Exit Conditions: Hold the short position for one day and close after the match.
Risk Management Rules: Short-selling strategy acts as a natural hedge to the equity market factor during bear markets.
Market Regime Suitability: Suitable as a hedge/diversification during bear markets.
Full Notes: Performance is estimated based on approximately 40 match days per year. Actual number of liquid stocks may vary.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Sentiment analysis, probability distribution bias, prediction markets, abnormal returns.

Strategy Description: A calendar anomaly suggesting that the equity market return in January predicts the performance for the remainder of the year (February to December).
Core Logic: The adage 'As goes January, so goes the rest of the year.' A strong January predicts a strong year, while a weak January predicts a weak year.
Entry Conditions: Invest in the equity market every January. If the January return is positive, stay invested in equities for the rest of the year.
Exit Conditions: If the January return is negative, switch the investment to T-Bills (or cash) for the remainder of the year.
Risk Management Rules: Market timing strategy that rotates into safe-haven assets (T-Bills) during predicted down years.
Market Regime Suitability: Not suitable as a hedge during crises as it remains long-only in equities during bull signals.
Full Notes: The strategy's alpha may be deteriorating, and the spread over passive investment is small. Caution is advised regarding data mining.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Calendar anomalies, data mining, predictive signaling, risk-adjustment.

Strategy Description: A well-known anomaly where small-cap stocks tend to perform exceptionally well during the month of January compared to other months.
Core Logic: Attributed to tax-loss selling; individual investors sell small-cap losers in December to claim capital losses and reinvest in January, driving prices up.
Entry Conditions: Invest in small-cap stocks at the beginning of January.
Exit Conditions: Switch investment to large-cap stocks for the remainder of the year (February to December).
Risk Management Rules: Long-only strategy; does not provide a hedge against market crises.
Long/Short Logic: Long-only (Small-cap in Jan, Large-cap rest of year).
Market Regime Suitability: Not suitable as a hedge.
Volatility Considerations: Not explicitly stated in source paper; QuantConnect backtest shows high drawdown.
Full Notes: The effect has shown a declining trend since 1988 and may be difficult to trade profitably after transaction costs.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Tax-loss selling hypothesis, behavioral finance, market capitalization deciles, Efficient Market Hypothesis challenge.

Category: Equities, Bonds, Commodities, Currencies, Momentum, Factor Investing, Smart Beta
Strategy Description: Unlike cross-sectional momentum, time series momentum focuses on an asset's own past return. The past 12-month excess return of an instrument is a positive predictor of its future return.
Core Logic: Consistent with behavioral theories of initial under-reaction and delayed over-reaction to information dissemination.
Entry Conditions: For each asset in the universe (e.g., 58 liquid futures), if the excess return over the past 12 months is positive, go long. If negative, go short.
Exit Conditions: Rebalance the portfolio monthly based on the updated 12-month excess returns.
Risk Management Rules: Position sizing is inversely proportional to the instrument's volatility to maintain a stable risk profile.
Long/Short Logic: Long/Short based on absolute return sign.
Market Regime Suitability: Acts as a hedge for extreme events; typically has negative correlation to equity market risk.
Full Notes: Performance is remarkably stable and robust across asset classes. Alpha is estimated using Fama-French factors.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Time series momentum, GARCH volatility modeling, risk parity, autocorrelation, under-reaction/over-reaction.

Strategy Name: Momentum Factor Combined with Asset Growth Effect
Category: Equities, Momentum, Stock Picking, Fundamental Analysis, Factor Investing, Smart Beta
Strategy Description: Combines the momentum effect with firm-level asset expansion. Momentum profits are found to be significantly stronger for stocks with high past asset growth.
Core Logic: Firm investment (asset growth) interacts with return continuation. The relationship remains strong even after adjusting for size, value, and volatility.
Entry Conditions: Sort stocks into deciles based on yearly asset growth. Within the highest growth decile, sort stocks into quintiles based on their past 11-month return (skipping the most recent month). Go long the top quintile.
Exit Conditions: Go short the bottom quintile of the high-growth group. Rebalance monthly and exclude January from trading.
Indicators Used: Asset growth (yearly change in total assets), 11-month price momentum.
Risk Management Rules: Strategy shows positive returns during recession months, providing diversification.
Market Regime Suitability: Suitable as a hedge during bear markets and recessions.
Full Notes: Performance excludes January, which is documented as a negative month for momentum strategies.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Fundamental analysis, factor interaction, cross-sectional sorting, asset expansion.

Strategy Name: 12 Month Cycle in Cross-Section of Stocks Returns
Category: Equities, Seasonality, Stock Picking, Factor Investing, Smart Beta
Strategy Description: Stocks with high historical returns in a specific calendar month tend to have high future returns in that same calendar month, creating a 12-month cycle.
Core Logic: Seasonal patterns of liquidity, compensation for systematic risk, or behavioral theories of investing drive recurring monthly performance.
Entry Conditions: Every month, group stocks into deciles according to their performance in the same month one year ago. Go long on the winner decile.
Exit Conditions: Go short on the loser decile. Rebalance the portfolio monthly.
Risk Management Rules: Long-short strategy; the short leg may potentially act as a hedge during market stress.
Market Regime Suitability: Unknown; requires rigorous backtesting to determine correlation to equity market risk.
Full Notes: The effect is independent of other known factors like value or momentum. Results are presented for large-cap stocks.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Cross-sectional seasonality, liquidity patterns, mood beta, systematic risk compensation.

Strategy Description: A strategy that times the US dollar against a basket of developed country currencies based on the average forward premium difference, capturing the US-specific compensation for bearing risk.
Core Logic: The strategy exploits the dollar risk premium. It uses the average forward discount (AFD) of a basket of currencies compared to the 3-month US Treasury rate to predict excess returns.
Entry Conditions: Go long on the US dollar and short on a basket of developed currencies if the 3-month US Treasury rate is higher than the AFD. Go short on the USD and long on the basket if the 3-month US Treasury rate is lower than the AFD.
Exit Conditions: The portfolio is rebalanced monthly based on the updated interest rate and AFD data.
Indicators Used: Average Forward Discount (AFD), 3-month US Treasury rate.
Risk Management Rules: Monthly rebalancing; diversification across a basket of 10 developed currencies.
Long/Short Logic: Long/Short based on the interest rate differential between the US and the average of the basket.
Market Regime Suitability: Counter-cyclical; performs well when US risk price is high, often during recessions.
Full Notes: The basket includes currencies from the Euro area, Australia, Canada, Denmark, Japan, New Zealand, Norway, Sweden, Switzerland, and the United Kingdom.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Forward discount/premium, interest rate parity, risk premium, counter-cyclicality.

Category: Stock Picking, Fundamental Analysis, Factor Investing
Strategy Description: Exploits the 'Conservative Minus Aggressive' (CMA) factor, where firms with low asset growth (conservative) outperform firms with high asset growth (aggressive).
Core Logic: Aggressive investments often fail to improve returns in the near future. Conservative firms with lower investment rates tend to yield higher risk-adjusted returns.
Entry Conditions: Stocks are sorted into five Size groups and five Investment (Inv) groups. Long the portfolio with the highest Size and lowest Investment; Short the portfolio with the highest Size and highest Investment.
Exit Conditions: Rebalanced yearly at the end of June.
Risk Management Rules: Value-weighted portfolios; independent sorting by size and investment.
Long/Short Logic: Long low-investment firms, Short high-investment firms.
Market Regime Suitability: Partially suitable as a hedge due to negative correlation with the broad equity market.
Volatility Considerations: Drawdown of -19.81% in the source paper; QuantConnect backtest shows 26% drawdown.
Full Notes: Based on the Fama-French five-factor model. Investment is defined as the growth of total assets for the fiscal year ending in t-1 divided by total assets at the end of t-1.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Asset growth, Fama-French factors, cross-sectional variance, value-weighting.

Strategy Description: A momentum strategy that ranks stocks based on residual returns from the Fama-French three-factor model to reduce systematic factor exposures and volatility.
Core Logic: Traditional momentum has time-varying exposures to systematic factors. Residual momentum removes these dependencies, leading to more consistent risk-adjusted profits.
Entry Conditions: Rank the 10% largest stocks by past 12-month residual returns (excluding the most recent month), standardized by the standard deviation of residuals. Long the top decile and Short the bottom decile.
Exit Conditions: Rebalanced monthly.
Indicators Used: Residual returns (from 36-month Fama-French regression), Standard deviation of residuals.
Risk Management Rules: Standardization of returns; factor neutrality; focus on large-cap stocks.
Long/Short Logic: Long top decile, Short bottom decile.
Market Regime Suitability: Hedge potential; shows positive returns during recession months (NBER defined).
Volatility Considerations: Estimated Volatility: 15.27%. Residual momentum has lower volatility than total return momentum.
Full Notes: Residual returns are estimated each month using a regression model over the past 36 months with Fama-French three factors as independent variables.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Multiple linear regression, Fama-French 3-factor model, standardization, residual returns.

Strategy Description: A market timing strategy that holds equities in Islamic countries during the month of Ramadan to exploit positive investor sentiment.
Core Logic: The upbeat mood and feelings of solidarity during Ramadan lead to optimistic investor behavior and significantly higher equity returns compared to the rest of the year.
Entry Conditions: Hold an equally weighted portfolio of ETFs from countries with a Muslim population > 50% during the month of Ramadan.
Exit Conditions: Exit to cash for the remainder of the year.
Risk Management Rules: Market timing (rotating out of equities); equal weighting.
Market Regime Suitability: Sentiment-driven; reduces overall equity risk by limiting exposure to a specific high-probability window.
Volatility Considerations: Returns during Ramadan are documented to be less volatile than during the rest of the year.
Full Notes: The strategy typically uses 14 Muslim countries. Ramadan is the ninth month of the Islamic lunar calendar.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Seasonality, investor sentiment, lunar calendar, market timing.

Strategy Description: A simple trend-following system applied to individual stocks using all-time highs as entry signals and ATR as a trailing stop.
Core Logic: Trend-following cuts the left tail of the return distribution. Buying stocks at new highs captures momentum while trailing stops manage risk.
Entry Conditions: Buy if today's close is greater than or equal to the highest close in the stock's entire history.
Exit Conditions: 10-period Average True Range (ATR) trailing stop.
Indicators Used: All-time high price, Average True Range (ATR).
Risk Management Rules: ATR trailing stop; liquidity and minimum price filters; equal weighting.
Market Regime Suitability: Not a hedge; highly correlated to the broad equity market.
Volatility Considerations: Estimated Volatility: 15.6%. ATR stop adapts to individual stock volatility.
Full Notes: Backtest includes 0.5% round-turn transaction costs. The strategy suggests trend-following works on stocks as well as futures.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Trailing stops, ATR, all-time highs, non-normal return distribution (fat tails).

Strategy Description: Applies the momentum anomaly to the universe of Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs), where past winners continue to outperform.
Core Logic: Behavioral biases like herding and under-reaction to news create persistence in REIT returns.
Entry Conditions: Rank all US REITs by past 11-month return (lagged one month). Go long the best performing tercile.
Exit Conditions: 3-month holding period.
Risk Management Rules: Equal weighting; tercile grouping; monthly rebalancing of 1/3 of the portfolio.
Long/Short Logic: Long only (though can be implemented as Long/Short).
Market Regime Suitability: Strong exposure to equity market risk; not suitable as a hedge.
Volatility Considerations: Momentum returns in REITs are higher when volatility is higher. Losers have higher idiosyncratic risk.
Performance Metrics (CAGR, Sharpe Ratio,: CAGR: 9.51%, Sharpe Ratio: Not stated, Max Drawdown: -55.36%
Full Notes: Recent studies suggest the REIT momentum effect may be dissipating or subsumed by earnings drift.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Behavioral biases, GARCH-in-mean model, idiosyncratic volatility.

Strategy Name: Momentum and Reversal Combined with Volatility Effect in Stocks
Strategy Description: Enhances momentum returns by focusing on high-volatility large-cap stocks, where information uncertainty intensifies return continuation.
Core Logic: Large-cap/high-volatility stocks exhibit momentum, while large-cap/low-volatility stocks exhibit reversals.
Entry Conditions: Filter for large-cap stocks (above size median). Rank by 6-month realized return and 6-month realized volatility. Long the highest performing quintile from the highest volatility group; Short the lowest performing quintile from the highest volatility group.
Exit Conditions: 6-month holding period.
Risk Management Rules: Equal weighting; size median filter; price > $5; 1/6 of portfolio rebalanced monthly.
Market Regime Suitability: Unknown; long leg is equity-correlated, short leg may provide partial hedge.
Volatility Considerations: Specifically targets high-volatility stocks to capture stronger momentum effects.
Momentum/Mean Reversion Classification: Momentum (in high vol) / Reversal (in low vol)
Full Notes: Skips one week prior to rebalancing to avoid microstructure biases. Works well within the large-cap universe.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Information uncertainty, behavioral under-reaction, realized volatility, quintile sorting.

Strategy Name: Momentum Effect in Stocks in Small Portfolios
Strategy Description: Demonstrates that the momentum effect can be effectively captured using a concentrated portfolio of as few as 20 stocks.
Core Logic: Momentum gains are strongest when using extreme winners and losers, allowing small investors to exploit the anomaly without needing hundreds of stocks.
Entry Conditions: Rank stocks by past 12-month performance. Long the 10 stocks with the highest performance and Short the 10 stocks with the lowest performance.
Exit Conditions: Rebalanced yearly.
Risk Management Rules: Equal weighting; exclusion of the bottom 25% market cap for liquidity.
Market Regime Suitability: Prone to 'momentum crashes' during market rebounds after large declines.
Volatility Considerations: Concentrated portfolios have higher idiosyncratic risk but capture extreme momentum.
Full Notes: Indicative performance assumes an account size of 10,000 pounds. Transaction costs (commissions, stamp duty, bid-ask spread) are significant for small portfolios.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Behavioral biases, under-reaction, momentum crashes, concentration risk.

Strategy Name: Exploiting Term Structure of VIX Futures
Strategy Description: Exploits the volatility premium by trading VIX futures based on the slope of the futures curve, hedged with S&P 500 futures.
Core Logic: Volatility is mean-reverting. The strategy sells VIX futures when the curve is in contango and buys when in backwardation.
Entry Conditions: Sell nearest VIX futures if in contango with a daily roll > 0.10. Buy if in backwardation with a daily roll < -0.10.
Exit Conditions: Hold for 5 trading days or exit if the daily roll reverts (e.g., < 0.05 for shorts).
Indicators Used: VIX futures price, Spot VIX, Daily Roll (basis / days to settlement).
Risk Management Rules: Hedged with E-mini S&P 500 futures using regression-based hedge ratios; 10-day minimum maturity filter.
Market Regime Suitability: Long VIX positions can hedge equity crises; short VIX positions are short-volatility and risky during crashes.
Volatility Considerations: Directly trades volatility; exploits the mean-reverting nature of the VIX.
Full Notes: The daily roll measures potential profits assuming the basis declines linearly until settlement. Alpha may be deteriorating in recent years.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Contango, backwardation, basis, OLS regression, hedge ratios.

Category: Stock Picking, Fundamental Analysis, Factor Investing
Strategy Description: A strategy that goes long on firms with high Return on Assets (ROA) and short on firms with low ROA, based on the q-theory of investment.
Core Logic: Firms with productive assets (high ROA) should yield higher average returns than firms with unproductive assets.
Entry Conditions: Divide stocks into two halves by market cap. Within each half, divide into deciles by ROA. Long the top 3 deciles and Short the bottom 3 deciles.
Exit Conditions: Rebalanced monthly.
Indicators Used: Return on Assets (ROA) = Quarterly Earnings / One-quarter-lagged Total Assets.
Risk Management Rules: Equal weighting; Sales > $10M filter; size-neutrality by sorting within market cap groups.
Market Regime Suitability: Unknown; long leg is equity-correlated, short leg may provide hedge during bad times.
Full Notes: The ROA effect is a key component of the q-factor model and can explain anomalies like earnings surprises and valuation ratios.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Q-theory of investment, productivity, return on assets, decile sorting.

Strategy Name: Value Factor - CAPE Effect within Countries
Strategy Description: A rotational strategy that periodically rotates to countries with the most undervalued equity markets based on Shiller's CAPE ratio.
Core Logic: Investors tend to overreact to news; 'winner' countries become overvalued while 'loser' countries become undervalued. The strategy exploits this by buying undervalued markets as they revert to intrinsic value.
Entry Conditions: Invest in the cheapest 33% of countries from the investment universe if those countries have a CAPE ratio below 15.
Exit Conditions: The portfolio is rebalanced yearly; exit positions that no longer fall within the cheapest 33% or whose CAPE exceeds 15.
Risk Management Rules: Equally weighted portfolio; hold 0% cash instead of countries with a CAPE higher than 15.
Timeframe: Long-term (5-10 years for valuation predictive power).
Market Regime Suitability: Not a hedge; high correlation to the broad equity market as it holds long equity positions.
Performance Metrics (CAGR, Sharpe Ratio,: CAGR: 14.7% (real return), Sharpe Ratio: 0.56, Max Drawdown: -27.17%.
Full Notes: Investment universe consists of 32 countries with accessible equity markets. CAPE is the ratio of real price to the 10-year average of real earnings.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Valuation ratios, Cyclical adjustment, Percentile sorting.

Strategy Description: A long-short strategy that targets stocks with high earnings quality, defined by multiple fundamental ratios, which tend to outperform low-quality stocks.
Core Logic: Investors often fixate on reported earnings without scrutinizing their quality. High-quality earnings (high cash flow, low leverage, high ROE) provide a persistent return premium.
Entry Conditions: Form a composite quality score (0-400) based on Cash Flow/Earnings, ROE, Cash Flow/Assets, and Debt/Assets. Go long the top 30% and short the bottom 30%.
Exit Conditions: Rebalanced yearly at the end of June.
Indicators Used: Cash flow relative to earnings, Return on Equity (ROE), Cash flow to total assets (CF/A), Debt to assets (D/A).
Risk Management Rules: Cap-weighting individual stocks within the long and short portfolios; composite scoring to diversify signal risk.
Market Regime Suitability: Positive returns during recession months; acts as a valuable diversifier to equity market risk.
Full Notes: Universe includes all non-financial stocks from NYSE, Amex, and Nasdaq. The strategy has low to negative correlation with value and momentum factors.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Composite scoring, Percentile ranking, Fundamental ratio analysis.

Strategy Description: Exploits the difference between the implied volatility of index options and the implied volatility of individual stock options.
Core Logic: The difference between implied and realized volatility is typically greater for index options than for individual stocks. The strategy sells index volatility and buys individual stock volatility.
Entry Conditions: Buy puts of stocks with the highest belief disagreement (analyst forecasts) and sell index puts with Black-Scholes deltas between -0.8 and -0.2.
Exit Conditions: Rebalanced monthly.
Indicators Used: Implied Volatility, Realized Volatility, Analyst forecast dispersion (I/B/E/S).
Risk Management Rules: Portfolio typically consists of 20 individual stock options and 1 index option position.
Long/Short Logic: Short index volatility, long individual stock volatility.
Market Regime Suitability: Not a hedge; loses money during stress periods when stock correlations rise (fat-tail risk).
Full Notes: Investment universe consists of S&P 100 index constituents. Belief disagreement is used as a proxy for priced risk in individual options.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Implied vs Realized Volatility, Correlation risk premium, Black-Scholes delta.

Strategy Description: Exploits an overreaction to the well-known Post-Earnings Announcement Drift (PEAD) anomaly in liquid stocks.
Core Logic: Investors have started to overreact to earnings news in easy-to-arbitrage stocks. Stocks with the worst returns during the previous earnings announcement tend to outperform at the next one.
Entry Conditions: Long the decile of stocks with the lowest abnormal performance during the previous earnings announcement; short the decile with the highest.
Exit Conditions: Stocks are held for two days around the earnings announcement.
Indicators Used: Abnormal performance during the previous earnings announcement.
Risk Management Rules: Equally weighted portfolio; focus on large-cap stocks with active options markets.
Market Regime Suitability: Unknown; the long leg is correlated to the market, while the short leg may provide some hedging.
Full Notes: The strategy targets firms where investors are now overcompensating for historical underreaction to earnings news.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Abnormal returns, Autocorrelation, Investor overreaction.

Strategy Name: Earnings Announcements Combined with Stock Repurchases
Strategy Description: Uses the timing of stock repurchase announcements as a signal for positive performance during subsequent earnings announcements.
Core Logic: Managers possess superior information and tend to announce stock repurchases when they expect positive earnings news. This timing reduces information asymmetry for investors.
Entry Conditions: Long stocks that announced a repurchase program (>=5% of shares) between 30 and 15 days before their earnings announcement date.
Exit Conditions: Positions are held from 10 days before to 15 days after the earnings announcement.
Indicators Used: Stock repurchase announcement date, Earnings announcement date.
Risk Management Rules: Equally weighted; exclude the bottom 25% of firms by market capitalization.
Long/Short Logic: Long-only (long-short version possible using SEO announcements).
Market Regime Suitability: Not a hedge; high correlation to the broad equity market due to long-only exposure.
Full Notes: Universe includes NYSE/AMEX/Nasdaq stocks, excluding ADRs, CEFs, and REITs.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Information asymmetry, Event study methodology.

Strategy Description: A strategy that buys commodities with low skewness and shorts those with high skewness based on the 'lottery-like' preference of investors.
Core Logic: Investors are willing to pay a premium for assets with high positive skewness (lottery-like payoffs), leading to lower expected returns for those assets.
Entry Conditions: Long the quintile of commodities with the 20% lowest total skewness and short the quintile with the 20% highest total skewness over a 12-month ranking period.
Exit Conditions: Rebalanced monthly.
Indicators Used: Total skewness (3rd moment of returns) calculated from daily returns over the past 12 months.
Risk Management Rules: Equally weighted; fully-collateralized portfolio using commodity futures.
Market Regime Suitability: Significantly negative correlation to the equity market; suitable as a hedge during bear markets.
Full Notes: Investment universe consists of 27 commodity futures contracts. The effect is not merely a consequence of retail investor preferences.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Skewness (3rd moment), Quintile sorting, Lottery-like payoff theory.

Strategy Description: An enhanced momentum strategy that buys 'consistent winners' and sells 'consistent losers' to improve the persistence of returns.
Core Logic: Stocks that are winners in both the formation month and the preceding period (consistent winners) outperform those that are only recent winners due to slower price adjustment.
Entry Conditions: Long stocks in the top decile of returns for both (t-7 to t-1) and (t-6 to t); short stocks in the bottom decile for both periods.
Exit Conditions: Positions are held for six months with no rebalancing during the period.
Risk Management Rules: Equally weighted; one-month skip between the formation and holding periods to avoid short-term reversal.
Market Regime Suitability: Unknown; long leg is correlated to the market, but the strategy is designed to be zero-investment.
Full Notes: Universe includes NYSE, AMEX, and NASDAQ stocks. Persistence is associated with size, idiosyncratic risk, and institutional ownership.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Duration analysis, Momentum persistence, Information asymmetry.

Strategy Description: A short-term reversal strategy that targets stocks around their earnings announcement dates.
Core Logic: Market makers demand higher compensation for inventory risks ahead of earnings announcements, leading to temporary price distortions that reverse after the news is released.
Entry Conditions: Sort large-cap stocks by returns in the window (t-4 to t-2). Long the bottom quintile (losers) and short the top quintile (winners).
Exit Conditions: Hold the stocks during the 3-day window (t-1, t, t+1) around the earnings announcement.
Indicators Used: 3-day window returns (t-4 to t-2), Earnings announcement date.
Risk Management Rules: Equally weighted; focus on the largest quintile of stocks by market capitalization for liquidity.
Market Regime Suitability: Unknown; long leg is correlated to the market, but the strategy targets specific event-driven reversals.
Full Notes: The reversal effect is six times stronger around earnings announcements than during random periods.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Inventory risk, Liquidity provision, Short-term reversal.

Strategy Name: Combining Fundamental FSCORE and Equity Short-Term Reversals
Strategy Description: Enhances the traditional short-term reversal strategy by using fundamental strength (FSCORE) to filter for higher-probability reversals.
Core Logic: Short-term reversals are driven by noise trading and investor underreaction to fundamental news. Buying losers with strong fundamentals exploits this underreaction.
Entry Conditions: Long past 1-month losers with an FSCORE >= 7; short past 1-month winners with an FSCORE <= 3.
Exit Conditions: Rebalanced monthly.
Indicators Used: Past 1-month returns, FSCORE (9 signals covering profitability, leverage, and efficiency).
Risk Management Rules: Equally weighted; focus on large stocks (top 40% of market capitalization).
Market Regime Suitability: Performs well during market crises as a 'liquidity providing' strategy; however, it is naturally 'short volatility'.
Full Notes: FSCORE is a nonparametric metric that synthesizes information from nine financial signals. The strategy outperforms unconditional reversal.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: FSCORE, Nonparametric metrics, Investor underreaction.

Strategy Description: A market timing strategy that buys the S&P 500 index on the 16th day of each month to capture the payday-driven investment flow.
Core Logic: Many companies pay employees on the 15th. Retirement contributions are processed and invested in the market on the following day (the 16th), creating upward pressure.
Entry Conditions: Buy the S&P 500 index at the end of the 15th day (or start of the 16th).
Exit Conditions: Hold the index during the 16th day and exit at the close.
Risk Management Rules: Long-only; timing the equity market factor for a single day.
Market Regime Suitability: Not a hedge; timing the equity market factor, so it remains exposed to broad market risk during the holding period.
Full Notes: The 16th day is statistically the 3rd best day of the month. The effect is linked to semi-monthly pay schedules.
Mathematical/Statistical Concepts: Calendar effects, Seasonality, Market microstructure.

Category: ESG Investing, Factor Investing, Smart Beta
Strategy Description: A long-short strategy that exploits the mispricing of corporate social performance by going long on stocks with high ESG scores and short on those with low ESG scores.
